title: 3.2.2 BF Brute Force Algorithm
subtitle: Matching process and complexity analysis, reserved for future visualization

content.introduction: The BF (Brute Force) algorithm compares the pattern at each possible text offset and is the simplest baseline for exact string matching.

sections.title: 3.2.2.1 Key Ideas and Model
sections.content: Define BF matching pointers and mismatch behavior. Explain why BF restarts comparisons from the next text position after mismatch.
examples: Use BF as a correctness baseline in tests

sections.title: 3.2.2.2 Representation and Workflow
sections.content: Walk through the character-by-character process, including offset updates, restart logic, and match completion criteria.
examples: Trace i and j pointer updates after mismatch
Find first and all matches by continuing scan
Handle empty pattern and short text edge cases

sections.title: 3.2.2.3 Complexity and Practical Guidance
sections.content: BF has worst-case O(nm), best-case near O(n) with early mismatches, and O(1) extra space. Discuss when this simplicity is acceptable.
examples: Estimate BF cost on repetitive text
Compare BF with KMP for long inputs
Prefer BF for tiny inputs or quick prototypes

keyTakeaways: Use BF for baseline validation and teaching
Know when to switch to linear-time matching
